name: Perceptions about Climate Change
description: Percentage of adults by their perception of climate change. Note: 2020 and 2021 data not available.

series.name: More of a problem for the future

name: Public Insight Monitor
description: Tracking poll on Scottish public attitudes to climate change, including concern and trust in the Scottish Government to act in Scotland's best interests.

series.name: Q1: Concerned about climate change in the year ahead

additionalQuestions.text: Q2: How much do you trust the Scottish Government to work in Scotland's best interests in relation to climate change?

name: Attitudes to nature
description: Scottish public attitudes to nature and the natural environment.

questions.text: The natural environment includes Scotland's landscapes, seas, coasts and wildlife. How important, if at all, do you think Scotland's natural environment is to the country as a whole?
How important is the natural environment to you personally?
How would you rate the condition of Scotland's nature?
Within your lifetime, do you think the state of Scotland's natural environment has improved or worsened?
Which of the following, if any, do you think poses the greatest risk to Scotland's nature? Please choose three.
To what extent are you concerned, if at all, about the threat of climate change and human activity to Scotland's natural environment?
How well or badly do you think the Scottish Government is doing in protecting nature?
At the upcoming UN Biodiversity Conference, a global target is expected to be agreed upon, working to protect 30% of land and sea by 2030. Which of the following statements is closest to your view?

options.label: Scotland should adopt this '30 by 30' target
Scotland should NOT adopt this '30 by 30' target

questions.text: The Scottish Government has set targets to plant more trees to help tackle climate change. On the right soils and with sensitive techniques, commercial forestry usually grows quicker and involves growing non-native tree species in an area. However, growing native species in woodlands has a greater biodiversity benefit and also reduces atmospheric carbon, albeit more slowly. Which of the following is closest to your view?

options.label: There should be a focus on growing native woodlands across Scotland's landscapes, even if it takes time to grow them
There should be a focus on growing non-native woodlands across Scotland's landscape, even if it reduces the biodiversity of these areas

questions.text: How important, if at all, is it to you personally that Scotland's seas are in a healthy state?
Which of the following do you think represent the greatest threat to the health of Scotland's seas? Please rank your top 3. (Sum % of Rank 1,2,3)

The Scottish government spends around half a billion pounds on farm funding annually. Many current farming practices cause pollution and severely deplete wildlife, as well as making farming a large source of greenhouse gas emissions. Next year the Scottish Government will create a new system of farm funding. How important is it to you that public spending on farming should support methods that restore nature and tackle climate change in addition to producing food?

Under the current system, many farms are entitled to "direct payments" from the Scottish government, based on the amount of land they own, which some argue provides a steady income away from dependence solely on food prices. Bigger farm businesses receive most of this money and there are few conditions as to how they farm the land. To what extent would you support or oppose capping direct payments available to farmers so there is more available to spend on climate and nature-friendly farming?

name: Urban and rural attitudes
description: Scottish public attitudes to nature and the environment, with rural and Highlands & Islands demographic breakdowns.

questions.text: To what extent, if at all, do you feel connected to the natural environment?
And how often do you visit nature (e.g., woodlands, coastal areas, lochs and rivers, local parks and gardens, etc.)?

options.label: NET: Once a week or more
NET: Less than once a week
NET: Once a week or more
NET: Less than once a week
NET: Once a week or more

questions.text: Are you a member of, or have you in the past 12 months made any donations to, any charities involved in nature conservation?
How well informed, if at all, do you think you are about issues affecting the natural environment?
Before today, how much, if anything, did you know about the following concepts? (% knowing a fair amount or more)
How often, if at all, do you worry about the natural environment (e.g., impacts of climate change, nature loss, etc.)?
How concerned, if at all, are you about the impact of climate change on food production in Scotland?
Have you taken or considered taking action to reduce your environmental impact, such as changing how you travel, what you eat, or what you buy?

options.label: — Have taken action and look to continue
— Have taken action but not looking to do more

questions.text: In your lifetime, have you noticed any of the following in your local area:
How vulnerable, if at all, do you believe the following types of communities are to the effects of climate change? (NET: Vulnerable)
Relating to actions you can take to help tackle climate change, where do you place yourself on the following scale?

options.label: 1 - I am highly motivated to take action on climate change
5 - I am not concerned about taking action on climate change

questions.text: Do you think that reaching Net Zero will be...

options.label: Neither good nor bad for Scotland

options.label: A better job than the Westminster government
About the same as the Westminster government
A worse job than the Westminster government

questions.text: To what extent, if at all, do you think that tackling climate change should be high on the agenda for the Scottish government?
Which of the following statements is closest to your view on what the Scottish Government should do when it comes to North Sea oil and gas drilling?

name: Environment and voting intention
description: Scottish public attitudes to the environment and climate change in the context of voting intention.

questions.text: On a scale from 0 to 10, where 0 is 'very unfavourable' and 10 is 'very favourable', how favourably do you view the following political parties? (NET: Favourable, 6–10)
Which of the following issues are most important to you in this election? (Select up to five)
To what extent do you agree or disagree? The next Scottish Government should clean up rivers and prevent pollution.
To what extent do you agree or disagree? The next Scottish Government should protect our seas from pollution, overfishing and damaging development.
To what extent do you agree or disagree? The next Scottish Government should do more to help farming be resilient in the face of climate impacts.
To what extent do you agree or disagree? The next Scottish Government should protect and restore Scotland's natural environment.
To what extent do you agree or disagree? The next Scottish Government should increase the extent of native woodlands.
To what extent do you agree or disagree? The next Scottish Government should increase investment in protecting the environment.
To what extent do you agree or disagree? The next Scottish Government should take stronger action to reduce emissions and tackle climate change.
How important are environmental and climate issues to you when deciding which party to vote for? (By 2024 UK General Election vote)
How likely would you be to vote for a party if you felt they were not willing to take action on environmental issues? (By 2024 UK General Election vote)
In general, how would you characterise each party's approach to environmental issues? (NET: Pro-environment)
In general, how would you characterise each party's approach to environmental issues? (NET: Anti-environment)
